items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: Which UK Act makes unauthorised access illegal?
items.why: It's Computer Misuse Act 1990. “Data Protection Act” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Personal data. “Software patents” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Computer Misuse Act passed in the UK?
items.why: Exact answer: 1990. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
